title: Visceral and Intrahepatic Fat Are Associated with Cardiometabolic Risk Factors Above Other Ectopic Fat Depots: The Framingham Heart Study

abstract:
<jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">Subclinical atherosclerosis identification remains challenging; abdominal visceral adiposity may improve risk stratification beyond traditional cardiovascular risk factors. Hypertriglyceridemic waist, a visceral adiposity marker combining elevated triglycerides (≥2 mmol/L) and waist circumference (≥90 cm), has been related to carotid atherosclerosis, although associations with high‐risk features, including lipid‐rich necrotic core (LRNC), remain unknown. We tested the hypothesis that hypertriglyceridemic waist is an independent marker of high‐risk atherosclerosis features.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Methods and Results</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">
              In this cross‐sectional study including 467 white men (mean age, 45.9±14.8 years; range 19.4–77.6 years), carotid atherosclerosis characteristics were examined by magnetic resonance imaging and associations with hypertriglyceridemic waist and benefits beyond Framingham Risk Score (FRS) and Pathobiological Determinants of Atherosclerosis in Youth (PDAY) were determined. Subclinical carotid atherosclerosis was present in 61.9% of participants, whereas 50.1% had LRNC. Hypertriglyceridemic waist was associated with carotid maximum wall thickness (
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.014), wall volume (
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.025), normalized wall index (
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.004), and Carotid Atherosclerosis Score (derived from wall thickness and LRNC;
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.049). Hypertriglyceridemic waist was associated with carotid LRNC volume beyond FRS (
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.037) or PDAY (
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.015), contrary to waist circumference alone (both
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              >0.05). Although 69.7% and 62.0% of participants with carotid atherosclerosis and/or LRNC were not high‐risk by FRS or PDAY, respectively, hypertriglyceridemic waist correctly reclassified 9.7% and 4.5% of them, respectively. Combining hypertriglyceridemic waist with FRS (net reclassification improvement=0.17;
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              <0.001) or PDAY (net reclassification improvement=0.05;
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              =0.003) was superior to each score alone in identifying individuals with carotid atherosclerosis and/or LRNC.
            </jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">Hypertriglyceridemic waist is an independent marker of carotid high‐risk atherosclerosis features in men, improving on FRS and PDAY risk score.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
title: Hypertriglyceridemic Waist: A Simple Marker of High‐Risk Atherosclerosis Features Associated With Excess Visceral Adiposity/Ectopic Fat

title: Study of visceral and subcutaneous abdominal fat thickness and its correlation with cardiometabolic risk factors and hormonal parameters in polycystic ovary syndrome
container-title: Indian Journal of Endocrinology and Metabolism

title: The global cardiometabolic risk: the role of ectopic fat deposition

abstract:
<jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">Gluteofemoral fat mass has been associated with improved cardiovascular disease risk factors. It is not clear if loss of this protective fat during weight loss partially negates the effect of loss of visceral fat. The aim of this study was to examine regional fat loss in a large weight‐loss cohort from one center and to determine if fat loss in the leg and total lean tissue loss is harmful.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Methods and Results</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">
              We combined the data from 7 of our previously published 3‐month weight‐loss studies and examined the relationship between regional fat and lean tissue loss and changes in cardiovascular disease risk factors in 399 participants. At baseline, leg fat was positively associated with high‐density lipoprotein cholesterol in women and inversely with fasting triglyceride level in both sexes. Abdominal lean tissue was also related to systolic blood pressure in men. Changes in regional fat and lean tissue were positively associated with changes in glucose, insulin, total cholesterol, triglycerides, low‐density lipoprotein cholesterol and systolic and diastolic blood pressure (
              <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
              =0.11–0.22,
              <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
              <0.05) with leg fat and arm lean tissue dominating in multivariate regression. After adjustment for total weight or total fat change, these relationships disappeared except for a positive relationship between arm and lean leg mass loss and changes in triglycerides and systolic blood pressure.
            </jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec xml:lang="en">
            <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
            <jats:p xml:lang="en">Loss of leg fat and leg lean tissue was directly associated with beneficial changes in cardiovascular disease risk markers. Loss of lean tissue may not have an adverse effect on cardiovascular disease risk, and measures to retain lean tissue during weight loss may not be necessary.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
title: Relationship Between Changes in Fat and Lean Depots Following Weight Loss and Changes in Cardiovascular Disease Risk Markers

affiliation.name: Alliance for Research in Exercise, Nutrition and Activity (ARENA), Sansom Institute for Health Research, School of Pharmacy and Medical Sciences, University of South Australia, Adelaide, Australia
